title: Java Intro
content:
Java Introduction

Write Once, Run Anywhere

Java is a high-level, object-oriented programming language used to build web, mobile, desktop, and enterprise applications. It is famous for being platform-independent; Java code gets compiled into bytecode, which can run on any device equipped with a Java Virtual Machine (JVM).

title: Hello World
content:
Hello World Program

Your First Program

Every Java application must contain a main() method. This is the entry point where the program begins executing.

Main.java
public class Main {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    // System.out.println prints text to the screen
    System.out.println("Hello World");
  }
}

title: Java Syntax
content:
Java Syntax

Strict Rules

Unlike JavaScript, Java is very strict. All code must be written inside a class, and the name of the file must exactly match the class name (e.g., Main.java). Furthermore, every single statement must end with a semicolon (;).

Syntax Rules
public class Main {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    System.out.println("I have a semicolon!");
  }
}

title: Data Types
content:
Variables & Data Types

Static Typing

Java is statically typed, meaning you must declare the data type of a variable before you can use it. Java has primitive types (simple values) and non-primitive types (objects).



Variable Declarations
public class Main {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    int num = 10;                // Integer (whole number)
    float price = 19.99f;        // Floating point number
    char letter = 'A';           // Single character
    boolean isJavaFun = true;    // True or false
    String name = "Sanjith";     // Text (Non-primitive)
    
    System.out.println(name + " is learning Java!");
  }
}

title: Operators
content:
Java Operators

Math and Logic

Operators perform operations on variables and values.

  • Arithmetic: +, -, *, /, % (Modulo/Remainder)
  • Assignment: =, +=, -=
  • Comparison: ==, !=, >, <
  • Logical: && (AND), || (OR), ! (NOT)

title: If/Else
content:
If/Else Statements

Control Flow

Java executes code from top to bottom. You can control this flow and make decisions using if, else if, and else statements.

Conditional Logic
public class Main {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    int age = 18;
    
    if (age >= 18) {
      System.out.println("You are an Adult.");
    } else {
      System.out.println("You are a Minor.");
    }
  }
}

title: Switch Case
content:
Switch Case

Multiple Conditions

Use the switch statement to select one of many code blocks to be executed. It is often a much cleaner alternative to writing many if/else statements.

Switch Example
public class Main {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    int day = 3;
    
    switch (day) {
      case 1:
        System.out.println("Monday");
        break;
      case 2:
        System.out.println("Tuesday");
        break;
      case 3:
        System.out.println("Wednesday");
        break; // break stops the code from running into the next case
      default:
        System.out.println("Other day");
    }
  }
}

title: Loops
content:
Java Loops

Repeating Code

Loops execute a block of code repeatedly as long as a specified condition is reached.



For & While Loops
public class Main {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    // For Loop: Used when you know exactly how many times to loop
    for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
      System.out.println("For Loop: " + i);
    }
    
    // While Loop: Loops as long as the condition remains true
    int j = 0;
    while (j < 3) {
      System.out.println("While Loop: " + j);
      j++;
    }
  }
}

title: Arrays
content:
Java Arrays

Fixed-Size Lists

Arrays are used to store multiple values in a single variable. In Java, traditional arrays have a fixed size, meaning once you set how many items it holds, you cannot change its size.

Array Syntax
public class Main {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    // Declare an array of Strings
    String[] cars = {"Volvo", "BMW", "Ford", "Mazda"};
    
    // Access items via their zero-based index
    System.out.println(cars[0]); // Outputs Volvo
    
    // Change an item
    cars[0] = "Opel";
    System.out.println(cars[0]); // Outputs Opel
  }
}

title: Methods
content:
Java Methods

Reusable Functions

A method (called a "function" in other languages) is a block of code that only runs when it is called. You can pass data (parameters) into a method, and it can return a result.

Defining Methods
public class Main {
  // Method that takes two ints and returns an int
  public static int add(int a, int b) {
    return a + b;
  }

  public static void main(String[] args) {
    int result = add(5, 3);
    System.out.println("5 + 3 = " + result);
  }
}

title: OOP Basics
content:
Classes & Objects

Object-Oriented Programming

Java is heavily Object-Oriented. A Class is a blueprint or template. An Object is a physical instance built from that blueprint. Each object has its own unique attributes and behaviors.



Class Instantiation
public class Car {
  // Attribute
  int speed;

  public static void main(String[] args) {
    // Create a new object from the Car blueprint
    Car myCar = new Car();
    myCar.speed = 50;
    
    System.out.println("My car is going " + myCar.speed + " mph.");
  }
}

title: Encapsulation
content:
Encapsulation

Hiding Data

Encapsulation is the concept of wrapping data (variables) and code acting on the data (methods) together as a single unit. You make variables private so they cannot be accessed directly from outside the class, and provide public getter and setter methods to control access safely.

Getters and Setters
public class Person {
  // Private data cannot be modified directly
  private int age;
  
  // Public Getter
  public int getAge() { 
    return age; 
  }
  
  // Public Setter (Can include validation logic!)
  public void setAge(int a) { 
    if (a > 0) {
      age = a; 
    }
  }
}

title: Inheritance
content:
Inheritance

Code Reusability

Inheritance allows one class (the subclass) to inherit fields and methods from another class (the superclass). We use the extends keyword to perform inheritance, which is great for code reusability.

The extends Keyword
class Animal {
  public void eat() {
    System.out.println("I can eat");
  }
}

// Dog inherits from Animal
class Dog extends Animal {
  public void bark() {
    System.out.println("Woof!");
  }
}

public class Main {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    Dog myDog = new Dog();
    myDog.eat();  // Inherited method!
    myDog.bark(); // Its own method
  }
}

title: Polymorphism
content:
Polymorphism

Many Forms

Polymorphism means "many forms", and it occurs when we have many classes that are related to each other by inheritance. It allows us to perform a single action in different ways (like method overriding).

Method Overriding
class Animal {
  public void animalSound() {
    System.out.println("The animal makes a sound");
  }
}

class Pig extends Animal {
  // Overriding the parent's method
  public void animalSound() {
    System.out.println("The pig says: wee wee");
  }
}

title: Abstraction
content:
Abstraction

Hiding Complexity

Data abstraction is the process of hiding certain details and showing only essential information to the user. Abstraction can be achieved with either abstract classes or interfaces.

Abstract Classes
// Abstract class (cannot be instantiated directly)
abstract class Animal {
  // Abstract method (does not have a body)
  public abstract void animalSound();
  
  // Regular method
  public void sleep() {
    System.out.println("Zzz");
  }
}

title: Exceptions
content:
Exception Handling

Catching Errors

When an error occurs, Java will normally stop and generate an error message. The try statement allows you to define a block of code to be tested for errors while it is being executed. The catch statement allows you to handle the error.

Try/Catch Block
public class Main {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    try {
      int[] myNumbers = {1, 2, 3};
      System.out.println(myNumbers[10]); // This will trigger an error!
    } catch (Exception e) {
      System.out.println("Something went wrong: " + e.getMessage());
    }
  }
}

title: User Input
content:
User Input with Scanner

Reading Data

To get user input in a Java console application, you can use the built-in Scanner class found in the java.util package.

Scanner Implementation
import java.util.Scanner;  // Import the Scanner class

public class Main {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    Scanner myObj = new Scanner(System.in);  // Create a Scanner object
    System.out.println("Enter username:");

    // Read user input
    String userName = myObj.nextLine();  
    System.out.println("Username is: " + userName);  
  }
}

title: String Methods
content:
Java String Methods

Manipulating Text

In Java, a String is actually an object, which means it contains methods that can perform certain operations on strings.

Common String Methods
public class Main {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    String txt = "Hello World";
    
    System.out.println("Length: " + txt.length());
    System.out.println("Upper: " + txt.toUpperCase());
    System.out.println("Lower: " + txt.toLowerCase());
    
    // Returns the index of the first occurrence of a specified text
    System.out.println("Index of World: " + txt.indexOf("World"));
  }
}

title: Collections
content:
Collections Framework

Dynamic Data Structures

Standard arrays in Java have a fixed size. The Collections Framework provides dynamic architectures (like ArrayList, HashMap, and LinkedList) that can grow or shrink automatically as you add or remove items.



ArrayList Example
import java.util.ArrayList;

public class Main {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    // Create a dynamic ArrayList that holds Strings
    ArrayList<String> cars = new ArrayList<String>();
    
    cars.add("Volvo");
    cars.add("BMW");
    cars.add("Ford");
    
    System.out.println(cars);
  }
}

title: Java Basics Summary
content:
Java Basics Summary

OOP Foundation Built

You have successfully learned the core principles of Java! You now understand strict syntax, static typing, and how control flow works.

  • Core Concepts: Variables, Loops, and the JVM.
  • Object-Oriented Programming (OOP): Classes, Inheritance, Polymorphism, and Encapsulation.
  • Advanced Tools: Exception handling and the dynamic Collections framework.

Let's continue to the next section where we will dive into advanced Java concepts like Multithreading, Streams, and File Handling!

title: Interfaces
content:
Java Interfaces

Contracts for Classes

An interface is a completely "abstract class" that is used to group related methods with empty bodies. Interfaces let multiple classes implement the same behaviors and help achieve true abstraction and multiple inheritance.

Implementing Interfaces
// Interface
interface Animal {
  public void animalSound(); // interface method (does not have a body)
  public void run(); // interface method (does not have a body)
}

// Dog "implements" the Animal interface
class Dog implements Animal {
  public void animalSound() {
    System.out.println("The dog says: woof");
  }
  public void run() {
    System.out.println("The dog is running.");
  }
}

title: Generics
content:
Java Generics

Type Safety

Generics allow you to write classes, interfaces, and methods that work with different data types while providing compile-time type safety (so you don't have to use specific casting). This makes your code safer and highly reusable.

Generic Class Example
// T stands for "Type"
class Box<T> {
  private T value;
  
  public void set(T val) {
    this.value = val;
  }
  public T get() {
    return value;
  }
}

public class Main {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    // Create a Box specifically for Strings
    Box<String> stringBox = new Box<>();
    stringBox.set("Hello Generics!");
    System.out.println(stringBox.get());
  }
}

title: File Handling
content:
File Handling

Managing Data

File handling is a crucial part of any backend application. The File class from the java.io package allows us to work with files and directories (create, read, update, delete).

Creating a File
import java.io.File;
import java.io.IOException;

public class Main {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    try {
      File myObj = new File("data.txt");
      if (myObj.createNewFile()) {
        System.out.println("File created: " + myObj.getName());
      } else {
        System.out.println("File already exists.");
      }
    } catch (IOException e) {
      System.out.println("An error occurred.");
      e.printStackTrace();
    }
  }
}

title: I/O Streams
content:
I/O Streams

Reading and Writing

A Stream is a sequence of data. In Java, I/O streams let you read and write data. Byte streams (like FileInputStream) handle 8-bit bytes (binary data like images), while Character streams (like FileReader) handle 16-bit Unicode characters (text).

Reading Text Files
import java.io.FileReader;
import java.io.IOException;

public class Main {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    try {
      FileReader fr = new FileReader("data.txt");
      int i;
      // Read character by character until the end of the file (-1)
      while ((i = fr.read()) != -1) {
        System.out.print((char) i);
      }
      fr.close();
    } catch (IOException e) {
      System.out.println("Error reading file.");
    }
  }
}

title: Collections Overview
content:
Collections Framework

Advanced Data Structures

The Java Collections Framework provides an architecture to store and manipulate a group of objects. It provides high-performance data structures like dynamic arrays, sets, and maps to manage data efficiently.



  • List: An ordered collection that can contain duplicate elements (e.g., ArrayList, LinkedList).
  • Set: A collection that cannot contain duplicate elements (e.g., HashSet).
  • Map: An object that maps keys to values (e.g., HashMap).

title: ArrayList
content:
ArrayList

Dynamic Arrays

The ArrayList class is a resizable array. The difference between a built-in array and an ArrayList is that the size of an array cannot be modified. However, elements can be added and removed from an ArrayList whenever you want.

ArrayList Methods
import java.util.ArrayList;

public class Main {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    ArrayList<Integer> nums = new ArrayList<Integer>();
    
    nums.add(10); // Adds 10
    nums.add(20); // Adds 20
    nums.add(30); // Adds 30
    
    nums.remove(0); // Removes the item at index 0 (which is 10)
    
    System.out.println("The first item is now: " + nums.get(0));
    System.out.println("Total size: " + nums.size());
  }
}

title: HashMap
content:
HashMap

Key-Value Pairs

A HashMap stores items in "key/value" pairs. You can access them by an index of another type (like a String). One object is used as a key (index) to another object (value). It provides incredibly fast data lookup.



HashMap Example
import java.util.HashMap;

public class Main {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    // Create a HashMap object called capitalCities
    HashMap<String, String> capitalCities = new HashMap<String, String>();

    // Add keys and values (Country, City)
    capitalCities.put("England", "London");
    capitalCities.put("Germany", "Berlin");
    capitalCities.put("Norway", "Oslo");

    // Fast lookup using the Key!
    System.out.println("Capital of Germany is: " + capitalCities.get("Germany"));
  }
}

title: Threads
content:
Java Threads

Multitasking

Threads allow a program to operate more efficiently by doing multiple things at the same time. They can be used to perform complicated tasks in the background without interrupting the main program execution.

Extending Thread
// Create a thread by extending the Thread class
class MyThread extends Thread {
  public void run() {
    System.out.println("This code is running in a separate thread!");
  }
}

public class Main {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    MyThread t = new MyThread();
    t.start(); // start() automatically calls the run() method
    System.out.println("This code is running in the main thread!");
  }
}

title: Synchronization
content:
Thread Synchronization

Preventing Conflicts

When multiple threads try to access or modify the same shared resource simultaneously, it can lead to data corruption or concurrency issues. Synchronization controls access by using the synchronized keyword to ensure that only one thread can access the resource at a time (Mutual Exclusion).

title: Advanced Summary
content:
Advanced Java Summary

Backend Powerhouse

By now, you have conquered the complex concepts that make Java a dominant language for enterprise-level applications.

  • Architecture: Interfaces for abstraction and Generics for safe, reusable code.
  • Data Structures: Mastering the dynamic Collections Framework (Lists, Sets, Maps).
  • System Operations: Reading/writing files using I/O Streams.
  • Performance: Handling simultaneous processes using Threads and Synchronization.

These concepts are absolutely essential for building real-world Java applications, REST APIs, and understanding deep object-oriented programming.

title: JDBC Intro
content:
Java JDBC Introduction

Database Connectivity

JDBC (Java Database Connectivity) is a standard Java API that connects your Java applications to relational databases (like MySQL, PostgreSQL, or Oracle). It acts as a bridge, translating Java calls into a language the database understands.



  • DriverManager: Manages a list of database drivers.
  • Connection: The active session with the database.
  • Statement: Used to execute SQL queries.
  • ResultSet: Holds the data retrieved from a database after a query.

title: JDBC Connection
content:
Establishing JDBC Connection

Connecting to Data

To connect to a database, you must load the specific database driver and use the DriverManager.getConnection() method. Note: Always remember to close your connections when finished to prevent memory leaks!

Connection Example
import java.sql.Connection;
import java.sql.DriverManager;
import java.sql.SQLException;

public class DBConnect {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    String url = "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/myDatabase";
    String user = "root";
    String password = "secret123";

    try {
      Connection conn = DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, password);
      System.out.println("Database connected successfully!");
      // conn.close(); // Close when done!
    } catch (SQLException e) {
      System.out.println("Connection failed: " + e.getMessage());
    }
  }
}

title: Executing Queries
content:
JDBC Executing Queries

Reading and Writing

Once connected, you use executeQuery() to read data (SELECT) and executeUpdate() to write data (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE). It is highly recommended to use PreparedStatement instead of a regular Statement to prevent SQL Injection attacks.

PreparedStatement Example
import java.sql.*;

public class QueryExample {
  public static void fetchUser(Connection conn, int userId) throws SQLException {
    // The '?' acts as a secure placeholder
    String sql = "SELECT username, email FROM Users WHERE id = ?";
    
    PreparedStatement pstmt = conn.prepareStatement(sql);
    pstmt.setInt(1, userId); // Safely inject the ID into the 1st placeholder
    
    ResultSet rs = pstmt.executeQuery();
    
    while(rs.next()) {
      System.out.println("User: " + rs.getString("username"));
    }
  }
}

title: Transactions
content:
JDBC Transactions

Ensuring Data Integrity

Transactions ensure that a group of changes to the database either all succeed together, or all fail together (like transferring money between two bank accounts). By default, JDBC commits every query instantly. You must turn this off to manage transactions manually.

Commit and Rollback
try {
  // 1. Turn off auto-commit
  conn.setAutoCommit(false);
  
  // 2. Execute multiple updates
  pstmt1.executeUpdate(); // Deduct from Account A
  pstmt2.executeUpdate(); // Add to Account B
  
  // 3. If both succeed, commit the transaction permanently
  conn.commit();
} catch (SQLException e) {
  // 4. If ANY error occurs, rollback the database to its previous state
  if (conn != null) {
    conn.rollback();
    System.out.println("Transaction failed. Data rolled back.");
  }
}

title: Servlets Intro
content:
Java Servlets

Server-Side Java

Java Servlets are server-side Java programs that handle HTTP requests from a client (like a web browser) and generate dynamic web content (like HTML or JSON) as a response.



When a browser sends a request, the web server (like Apache Tomcat) hands the request to the Servlet Container, which executes the Java code and sends the response back over the internet.

title: Servlet Life Cycle
content:
Servlet Life Cycle

Managed by the Server

A servlet's life cycle is entirely managed by the server container. It goes through three main phases, which allows you to efficiently initialize and release resources like database connections.



  • init(): Called exactly once when the servlet is first loaded into memory.
  • service(): Called every single time a new HTTP request is received. It directs the request to the appropriate method (like doGet or doPost).
  • destroy(): Called exactly once before the servlet is shut down, used to clean up memory.

title: Handling Requests
content:
Handling Servlet Requests

GET and POST

Servlets handle specific HTTP methods by overriding doGet() (for reading data) and doPost() (for submitting data). You can read data sent by the user using request.getParameter(), and send data back using response.getWriter().

A Basic Servlet
import java.io.*;
import javax.servlet.*;
import javax.servlet.http.*;

public class HelloServlet extends HttpServlet {
  // Handle a GET request
  protected void doGet(HttpServletRequest request, HttpServletResponse response) throws ServletException, IOException {
    
    // Read a URL parameter (e.g., ?name=Sanjith)
    String userName = request.getParameter("name");
    
    // Set response type and write HTML back to the browser
    response.setContentType("text/html");
    PrintWriter out = response.getWriter();
    out.println("<h1>Hello, " + userName + "!</h1>");
  }
}

title: JSP Intro
content:
JavaServer Pages (JSP)

Dynamic Web Pages

Writing massive HTML files inside Java out.println() statements (like we did in pure Servlets) is messy and difficult to maintain. JavaServer Pages (JSP) is a server-side technology that solves this by allowing you to embed Java code directly inside your HTML files.

Under the hood, the server actually takes your JSP file and compiles it into a regular Java Servlet before sending it to the browser!

title: JSP Syntax
content:
Basic JSP Syntax

Java Inside HTML

JSP files (saved with a .jsp extension) look just like normal HTML, but you can inject Java logic using special tags.

  • Scriptlets <% ... %>: Used to write block Java code (like loops or logic).
  • Expressions <%= ... %>: Used to output a Java variable directly to the HTML page.
index.jsp
<html>
<body>
  <h2>Welcome to my JSP Page!</h2>
  
  <!-- A Java Expression -->
  <p>The current time is: <%= new java.util.Date() %></p>

  <!-- A Java Scriptlet -->
  <ul>
    <% for(int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) { %>
      <li>Item number <%= i %></li>
    <% } %>
  </ul>
</body>
</html>

title: JSP vs Servlet
content:
JSP vs Servlet

The Perfect Pair (MVC)

In modern Java web development, you don't choose between JSP and Servlets—you use them together! Servlets are pure Java classes, making them perfect for business logic and database calls. JSP is higher level, making it perfect for the presentation layer (UI).



This is the foundation of the Model-View-Controller (MVC) pattern: The Servlet (Controller) fetches data from the database, and passes it to the JSP (View) to be displayed beautifully to the user.

title: Java EE Overview
content:
Java Enterprise Edition

Enterprise Architecture

Java Enterprise Edition (now officially known as Jakarta EE) is a massive set of specifications extending standard Java SE. It is designed specifically for building large-scale, distributed, and multi-tiered web applications.

  • Includes standardized APIs for web services, persistence, messaging, and security.
  • Designed to simplify enterprise development so developers can focus on business logic rather than infrastructure.
  • Powered by robust application servers like Apache Tomcat, JBoss, and GlassFish.

title: JavaBeans
content:
JavaBeans & Managed Beans

Standardized Objects

A JavaBean is simply a standard Java class that follows strict conventions so it can be easily reused and manipulated by frameworks. A Managed Bean is a JavaBean whose lifecycle is completely controlled by a server container (like JSF or Spring).

JavaBean Rules
import java.io.Serializable;

// 1. Must implement Serializable
public class UserBean implements Serializable {
  
  // 2. Properties must be private
  private String username;
  
  // 3. Must have a public no-argument constructor
  public UserBean() {}
  
  // 4. Must provide public getters and setters
  public String getUsername() { return username; }
  public void setUsername(String username) { this.username = username; }
}

title: JAX-RS (REST)
content:
JAX-RS – RESTful APIs

Modern Web Services

JAX-RS is the official Java API for building RESTful web services. It simplifies creating backend endpoints that communicate with frontend applications (like React or Vue) using JSON or XML over standard HTTP protocols.



REST Controller Example
import javax.ws.rs.*;
import javax.ws.rs.core.MediaType;

// Maps this class to the "/users" URL
@Path("/users")
public class UserResource {
  
  // Listens for HTTP GET requests
  @GET
  @Produces(MediaType.APPLICATION_JSON)
  public String getUsers() {
    // In a real app, this fetches users from a database and returns JSON
    return "[{ \"name\": \"Sanjith\", \"role\": \"Admin\" }]";
  }
}

title: JAX-WS (SOAP)
content:
JAX-WS – SOAP Web Services

Legacy Integrations

JAX-WS is the Java API for building SOAP-based web services. Unlike REST (which usually uses lightweight JSON), SOAP relies entirely on strict, heavy XML envelopes. While REST is the modern standard, SOAP is still heavily used in highly secure enterprise systems, banking, and legacy corporate integrations.

title: MVC Pattern
content:
MVC Pattern in Java Web Apps

Separation of Concerns

The Model-View-Controller (MVC) pattern separates an application into three interconnected components. Frameworks like Spring MVC and Jakarta MVC use this exact architecture to build highly scalable and maintainable enterprise applications.



  • Model: Represents the data and the core business logic (JavaBeans, Database Entities).
  • View: The user interface and presentation layer (HTML, JSP, React).
  • Controller: Handles routing, listens to user requests, updates the Model, and returns the View (Servlets, REST Controllers).

title: JPA (ORM)
content:
Java Persistence API (JPA)

Object-Relational Mapping

Writing raw JDBC SQL code is tedious. JPA is a standard API for Object-Relational Mapping (ORM). Implementations of JPA (like Hibernate) allow you to map Java classes directly to database tables, avoiding low-level SQL entirely.



JPA Entity Example
import javax.persistence.*;

@Entity // Tells JPA to map this class to a database table
@Table(name = "employees")
public class Employee {
  
  @Id // Marks this as the Primary Key
  @GeneratedValue(strategy = GenerationType.IDENTITY)
  private Long id;
  
  @Column(name = "first_name")
  private String firstName;
  
  // Getters and Setters omitted for brevity
}

title: Dependency Injection
content:
Dependency Injection (DI)

Inversion of Control

DI is a powerful design pattern. Instead of a class manually creating the objects it needs using the new keyword, an external container (like the Spring Framework) automatically creates and "injects" those dependencies at runtime. This promotes loosely coupled, easily testable code.



Spring @Autowired Example
import org.springframework.beans.factory.annotation.Autowired;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Service;

@Service
public class UserService {
  
  private final UserRepository userRepository;
  
  // Spring automatically injects the repository here!
  // We never have to write "new UserRepository()"
  @Autowired
  public UserService(UserRepository userRepository) {
    this.userRepository = userRepository;
  }
}

title: Filters & Listeners
content:
Servlet Filters & Listeners

Intercepting Requests

Filters and Listeners act as the security guards and monitors of your web application.



  • Filters: Intercept HTTP requests before they reach the Servlet, and responses before they reach the user. Used heavily for user authentication, data compression, and logging.
  • Listeners: Monitor background lifecycle events, such as triggering code exactly when a server starts up, or when a new user session is created.

title: Session Management
content:
Session Management

Remembering Users

HTTP is a "stateless" protocol, meaning the server forgets who you are the second after it sends you a web page. Java web apps manage user state across multiple HTTP requests using Sessions.

HTTPSession API
protected void doGet(HttpServletRequest request, HttpServletResponse response) {
  
  // Retrieve the current session, or create a new one if it doesn't exist
  HttpSession session = request.getSession();
  
  // Store user data on the server securely
  session.setAttribute("username", "Sanjith_Admin");
  
  // The server sends a "Session ID" cookie back to the user's browser.
  // Next time the user clicks a link, the browser sends that ID back so 
  // the server remembers them!
}

title: Web Security
content:
Java Web Security Basics

Locking Down the App

Security is non-negotiable in enterprise apps. Frameworks like Spring Security are used to automatically enforce strict rules.

  • HTTPS: Encrypt all data traveling between the user and the server.
  • SQL Injection: Never concatenate raw strings to database queries. Always use PreparedStatements or JPA.
  • XSS (Cross-Site Scripting): Always sanitize and escape user input before rendering it back onto an HTML page.
  • Authentication/Authorization: Ensure users are who they claim to be, and strictly control which roles (e.g., Admin vs User) can access certain URLs.
